Title: Ci Wara Headdress
Description: Among the Bamana people of Mali, oral traditions credit the mythical figure Ci Wara - a half human, half antelope being- with teaching humanity the principles of agriculture. Ci Wara headdresses are traditionally worn in pairs during ceremonial performances connected to planting and harvesting seasons. The styled antelope imagery symbolizes discipline, labor, and the spiritual relationship between humans and the land. The elongated horns and abstract geometric carving reflect Bamana artistic traditions emphasizing rhythm, movement, and symbolic abstraction rather than naturalistic representation. These performances reinforce cultural knowledge, communal identity, and respect for agricultural labor within Bamana Society.
Creator: Unknown Bamana Artist
Subject: agriculture; mythology; fertility; performance; subsistence farming

function_use: Used in ceremonial agricultural dances performed by members of the Ci Wara society to honor he mythical being Ci Wara society to honor the mythical being Ci Wara and encourage successful farming, fertility, and communal prosperity.
provenance: Part of the Jackson State University Permanent African Art Collection. Exact acquisition history currently unknown.
acquisition_context: Collected for educational, research, and exhibition purposes within the Department of Art at Jackson State University.
cultural_sensitivity_note: This object represents a sacred ceremonial tradition connected to Bamana cutural identity, spirituality, and agricultural knowledge. Interpretive materials should acknowledge the object's ceremonial significance and avoid reducing it to decorative art alone.

Title: Untitled object 2
Description: This standing power figure is attributed to the Kongo cultural region and reflects the tradition of nkisi figures created to mediate between the human and spiritual realms. Such figures were activated through ritual practices conducted by ritual specialists and were believed to house spiritual forces capable of protection, healing, oath enforcement, and conflict resolution. The prominent abdominal bundle likely once contained sacred substances that endowed the figure with efficacy. The composed facial expression, upright posture, and material additions emphasize restraint, vigilance, and latent spiritual authority rather than overt aggression.
Subject: spiritual power; protection; healing; ancestral mediation; ritual practice

Title: Tabwa Mask
Description: This mask is attributed to the Tabwa people and reflects Central African traditions in which masks function as instruments of moral authority and ancestral presence. Characterized by geometric facial segmentation, narrow eyes, and a restrained expression, the mask emphasizes composure, wisdom, and social discipline rather than individual portraiture. Such masks were typically used in ceremonial contexts connected to initiation, funerary rites, or social regulation, where performance activated their meaning. The incised surface and dark patina suggest sustained ritual use and reinforce the mask’s role as a mediator between the living community and the ancestral realm.
Subject: ancestral authority; moral instruction; initiation; funerary practices; performance

Title: Tabwa Buffalo Slit Drum
Description: This wooden slit drum is carved in the form of a buffalo, an animal associated with strength, endurance, and social authority in Central African cultures. Among the Tabwa, such drums were used for communication and ritual purposes, including signaling, ceremonial performances, and community gatherings. The elongated body functions as a resonating chamber, while the carved zigzag motifs and stylized animal features emphasize both visual symbolism and acoustic function. The drum reflects the integration of sound, sculpture, and social meaning in Tabwa artistic traditions.
Subject: communication; ritual sound; buffalo symbolism; community gatherings; social authority

Title: Tabwa Buffalo Mask
Description: This carved wooden mask represents a buffalo, an animal associated among the Tabwa with strength, endurance, and controlled social authority. Buffalo imagery conveys moral power exercised with restraint and is often linked to protection, communal responsibility, and the enforcement of social norms. Such masks were used in ceremonial performances where animal symbolism embodied ancestral presence and ethical discipline within the community.

Title: Untitled object 6
Description: This mask is a male kifwebe associated with the Songye people and the Bwadi Bwa Kifwebe society. Characterized by an elongated form, half-closed eyes, pronounced facial planes, and a raffia beard, male kifwebe masks embody aggressive spiritual power used to enforce social order. Such masks appeared in masquerades addressing conflict, discipline, and communal regulation, where movement and costume amplified their intimidating presence.
Subject: social regulation; ritual enforcement; spiritual power; masquerade; performance

Title: Bembe Ibulu Iya (Janus Mask)
Description: This mask is attributed to the Bembe people and reflects Central African masking traditions associated with initiation, healing, and social transition. Characterized by a rounded form, large circular eye surrounds, and red and white painted facial zones, the mask emphasizes visual contrast and presence rather than aggressive abstraction. Raffia elements formed part of the masquerade costume, activating the mask through movement and performance within community rituals.
Subject: initiation; healing; masquerade; social transition; performance

Title: Untitled object 8
Description: This object is a lukasa, a memory board used by the Luba people as a mnemonic device for preserving and transmitting historical knowledge. Lukasa were employed by members of the Mbudye society to recall genealogies, political authority, moral teachings, and significant events. The incised surface patterns and sculpted seated figure functioned as tactile and visual prompts activated through recitation and instruction rather than visual reading alone.
Subject: historical memory; political authority; oral tradition; mnemonic device; governance

Title: Untitled object 9
Description: This standing female figure is attributed to the Baule people and represents a blolo bla, or female spirit spouse. Such figures embody ideals of beauty, balance, and spiritual composure and were maintained in personal shrines to honor spirit-world partners believed to affect health, fertility, and social wellbeing. The elongated neck, serene facial expression, and naturalistic proportions reflect Baule aesthetic values and beliefs about harmony between the human and spiritual realms.
Subject: spirit spouse; fertility; spiritual harmony; personal shrine; idealized beauty

Title: Kifwebe Mask
Description: This mask is attributed to the Songye people and is associated with the kifwebe masking tradition. Characterized by a tall central crest, narrow eye slits, and an elongated face, the mask embodies authoritative spiritual power linked to social regulation. Such masks were worn by members of the Bwadi Bwa Kifwebe society in performances intended to enforce moral order, address conflict, and maintain communal discipline.

Title: Untitled object 11
Description: This banda (also known as kumbaruba) headdress represents a powerful composite being combining human and animal forms. The elongated horizontal structure incorporates a human face with Baga scarification and an elaborately braided female coiffure, joined to the jaw of a crocodile with visible teeth, along with antelope horns, a serpent’s body, and a chameleon’s tail. Historically a sacred figure associated with protection and male initiation rites, banda was accessible only to senior elders. Today it is performed in masquerades accompanied by drumming and dynamic choreography that evokes animal movement.
Subject: masquerade; animal symbolism; initiation; protection; performance

Title: Kifwebe Mask
Description: A male kifwebe mask featuring a prominent sagittal crest and deep linear incisions filled with white pigment. The mask's aggressive geometry and protruding features are designed to embody the mystical power of the bwadi bwa kifwebe society. It functioned in rituals of social regulation and political consolidation.

Title: Dogon Ritual Vessel Support
Description: This Dogon ritual vessel support features two kneeling anthropomorphic figures facing one another while jointly supporting a bowl or container. The symmetrical composition, elongated limbs, and stylized facial features reflect characteristic Dogon sculptural aesthetics associated with spiritual mediation and ceremonial practice. The horn-like coiffures, layered neck adornments, and matte brown patina suggest repeated ritual handling and long-term ceremonial use. Among the Dogon peoples of Mali, carved figures often function as intermediaries between ancestral forces, spiritual knowledge, and community life. The balanced posture of the paired figures may symbolize duality, harmony, collective responsibility, or ritual partnership within Dogon cosmology.
Creator: Unknown Dogon artist
Subject: Ritual vessel support; offerings; communal rites; mediation with ancestral and spiritual forces
Publisher: Jackson State University. (electronic version); JSUMS ART. (electronic version)
Notes: Visual analysis suggests ceremonial rather than decorative function. Identification based on stylistic similarities to Dogon ritual support figures from the Bandiagara Escarpment region.
Disclaimer: All rights reserved. Reproduction or redistribution requires written permission from Jackson State University.
Collection: Jackson State University African Art Collection.
Transcript: Paired kneeling anthropomorphic figures supporting a ritual vessel. Dogon culture, Mali. Carved wood. Late 19th–early 20th century.
technique: Hand-carved wood sculpture with incised detailing
function_use: This sculptural support figure likely functioned as a ceremonial vessel holder used during ritual offerings, ancestral observances, or communal spiritual ceremonies. The paired figures emphasize cooperation, balance, and mediation between the human and spiritual realms.
provenance: Part of the Jackson State University Permanent African Art Collection. Specific acquisition history currently unknown.
acquisition_context: Collected for educational study, exhibition, and research within the Jackson State University Department of Art.
cultural_sensitivity_note: Dogon ritual objects often carry ceremonial and spiritual significance connected to ancestral traditions and sacred community practices. Interpretive language should acknowledge the object’s cultural and ritual context rather than presenting it solely as decorative sculpture.
